truth.papers.A36.title: Enhancing strength and ductility in Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C austenitic low-density steels through Ni and Cr alloying synergy adding
truth.papers.A36.paper_scope: Design and investigation of Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.5C-xNi-yCr austenitic low-density steels with improved strength-ductility synergy through Ni and Cr alloying and thermomechanical processing, unraveling the role of nanoscale precipitates and deformation mechanisms.

prediction.papers.A36.title: Enhancing strength and ductility in Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C austenitic low-density steels through Ni and Cr alloying synergy adding
prediction.papers.A36.paper_scope: Design of Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.5C austenitic low-density steels by Ni and Cr synergistic alloying with thermo-mechanical processing and ultrafast cooling, elucidating precipitation control of nanoscale κ-carbides and LRO domains, deformation mechanisms (HDDA and DSBR), and strength-ductility synergy.

truth.processes.proc_ufc_3cr.description: Homogenization at 1200°C for 2h, hot rolling from 1040-1060°C to 950-970°C with 95% reduction to 4 mm thickness, followed by ultrafast cooling to ambient temperature at ~40°C/s using ADCOS-PM device.
truth.processes.proc_ufc_2ni.description: Homogenization at 1200°C for 2h, hot rolling from 1040-1060°C to 950-970°C with 95% reduction to 4 mm thickness, followed by ultrafast cooling to ambient temperature at ~40°C/s using ADCOS-PM device.
truth.processes.proc_ufc_2ni3cr.description: Homogenization at 1200°C for 2h, hot rolling from 1040-1060°C to 950-970°C with 95% reduction to 4 mm thickness, followed by ultrafast cooling to ambient temperature at ~40°C/s using ADCOS-PM device.
truth.processes.proc_ufc_2ni5cr.description: Homogenization at 1200°C for 2h, hot rolling from 1040-1060°C to 950-970°C with 95% reduction to 4 mm thickness, followed by ultrafast cooling to ambient temperature at ~40°C/s using ADCOS-PM device.

prediction.processes.proc_ufc_3cr.description: Vacuum induction melting, homogenization at 1200°C for 2 h, hot-rolled from 1040–1060°C to 950–970°C to 4 mm-thick sheets with 95% thickness reduction, then online ultrafast cooled (UFC) to ambient temperature at ~40°C/s.
prediction.processes.proc_ufc_2ni.description: Vacuum induction melting, homogenization at 1200°C for 2 h, hot-rolled from 1040–1060°C to 950–970°C to 4 mm-thick sheets with 95% thickness reduction, then online ultrafast cooled (UFC) to ambient temperature at ~40°C/s.
prediction.processes.proc_ufc_2ni3cr.description: Vacuum induction melting, homogenization at 1200°C for 2 h, hot-rolled from 1040–1060°C to 950–970°C to 4 mm-thick sheets with 95% thickness reduction, then online ultrafast cooled (UFC) to ambient temperature at ~40°C/s.
prediction.processes.proc_ufc_2ni5cr.description: Vacuum induction melting, homogenization at 1200°C for 2 h, hot-rolled from 1040–1060°C to 950–970°C to 4 mm-thick sheets with 95% thickness reduction, then online ultrafast cooled (UFC) to ambient temperature at ~40°C/s.

prediction.interfaces.sample_3cr.phase_evolution: Cr addition increases nucleation energy barrier, suppressing κ-carbide growth and favoring smaller LRO domains.
prediction.interfaces.sample_2ni.defect_interaction: Moving dislocations shear the κ-carbides; shear strengthening is the predominant precipitation strengthening mechanism.
prediction.interfaces.sample_2ni.phase_evolution: Ni addition reduces nucleation energy barrier via AlNi3C formation, promoting κ-carbide nucleation and growth.
prediction.interfaces.sample_2ni3cr.defect_interact: Moving dislocations shear the κ-carbides; shear strengthening is the predominant precipitation strengthening mechanism.
prediction.interfaces.sample_2ni3cr.phase_evolution: Combined Ni and Cr synergistically control precipitate size between pure Ni and pure Cr effects.
prediction.interfaces.sample_2ni5cr.phase_evolution: Higher Cr content further suppresses κ-carbide formation, resulting in finer LRO domains compared to 3Cr.

prediction.properties.sample_3cr.mechanical.tensile: Engineering stress-strain and true stress-strain curves shown in Fig. 4a and 4b; strain hardening curve in Fig. 4c shows three stages (I, II, III).
prediction.properties.sample_2ni.mechanical.tensile: Engineering stress-strain and true stress-strain curves shown in Fig. 4a and 4b; strain hardening curve in Fig. 4c shows three stages (I, II, III).
prediction.properties.sample_2ni3cr.mechanical.tens: Engineering stress-strain and true stress-strain curves shown in Fig. 4a and 4b; strain hardening curve in Fig. 4c shows three stages (I, II, III).
prediction.properties.sample_2ni5cr.mechanical.tens: Engineering stress-strain and true stress-strain curves shown in Fig. 4a and 4b; strain hardening curve in Fig. 4c shows three stages (I, II, III).

truth.characterization_methods.sample_3cr.purp: Microstructure characterization, phase identification, grain size measurement, precipitate analysis
truth.characterization_methods.sample_3cr.key_: Fully recrystallized single γ phase, average grain size 7.63 μm (without twins), 3.51 μm (with twins); LRO domains ~1.75 nm, no κ-carbides; faint superlattice reflections corresponding to LRO.
truth.characterization_methods.sample_2ni.purp: Microstructure characterization, phase identification, grain size measurement, precipitate analysis
truth.characterization_methods.sample_2ni.key_: Fully recrystallized single γ phase, average grain size 8.95 μm (without twins), 3.73 μm (with twins); orthorhombic κ-carbides ~10.69 nm, cuboidal morphology; superlattice reflections from κ.
truth.characterization_methods.sample_2ni3cr.p: Microstructure characterization, phase identification, grain size measurement, precipitate analysis
truth.characterization_methods.sample_2ni3cr.k: Fully recrystallized single γ phase, average grain size 8.57 μm (without twins), 3.72 μm (with twins); κ-carbides ~5.46 nm; superlattice reflections from κ.
truth.characterization_methods.sample_2ni5cr.p: Microstructure characterization, phase identification, grain size measurement, precipitate analysis
truth.characterization_methods.sample_2ni5cr.k: Fully recrystallized single γ phase, average grain size 7.60 μm (without twins), 2.2 μm (with twins); LRO domains ~2.14 nm; faint superlattice reflections, no satellite spots.

truth.computational_details.idx_0.simulation_m: First-principles DFT calculations using Materials Project database, GGA-PBE functional
truth.computational_details.idx_0.validation_a: Formation energies compared with experimental precipitate formation trends; strengthening model validated against measured yield strengths.
truth.computational_details.idx_0.analytical_m: Predict yield strength by summing frictional stress, solid solution, grain boundary, and precipitation strengthening components; precipitate shearing mechanism considered via ordering, coherency, and modulus mismatch.

Taylor factor M=3.06, shear modulus G=70 GPa, Burgers vector b=0.264 nm, antiphase boundary energy γ_APB=0.35 J/m^2, lattice parameter mismatch ε_c=0.9%, strong obstacle factor A_s=0.64, constants α_ε=2.6, m=0.85; precipitate size distributions and volume fractions from TEM measurements, carbon contents from chemical analysis.
Described in Equations (1)–(4). Precipitate strengthening dominant mechanism determined by comparing σ_order and σ_coh+σ_mod. Good agreement between predicted and experimental yield strengths.

prediction.computational_details.idx_0.simulation_m: Density functional theory (DFT) using generalized gradient approximation (GGA) with Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) functional
prediction.computational_details.idx_0.validation_a: Predicted yield strengths from analytical strengthening model agree very well with tensile test results (Fig. 11); calculated formation energies explain observed κ-carbide/LRO size trends.
prediction.computational_details.idx_0.analytical_m: Yield strength superposition (σys = σ0 + σss + σgb + σprep)
Predict yield strength from solid solution, grain boundary, and precipitation strengthening contributions.
Precipitation strengthening treated by shearing mechanism (ordering, coherency, modulus mismatch); transition from ordering to coherency+modulus dominance depends on precipitate radius (Fig. 10).

truth.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: Fracture surfaces of all four steels exhibit numerous dimples, indicating ductile fracture mode (Fig. 5).
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: Engineering stress-strain curves show that total elongation increases with Cr content, while yield strength increases with Ni content; the 2Ni3Cr steel achieves YS>1100 MPa, UTS>1200 MPa, TEL>45%, and a TS×TEL product of 55.36 GPa·%.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Strain hardening rate curves exhibit three stages: Stage I (sharp decrease), Stage II (increase, prolonged with Cr), Stage III (decrease); 2Ni5Cr shows higher hardening rate than 2Ni and 2Ni3Cr in Stage III.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Deformation mechanisms differ: 3Cr steel shows HDDA and dynamic slip band refinement (DSBR) with few deformation twins near fracture; 2Ni steel is dominated by planar slip, no deformation twins, only stacking faults at very high strain.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: First-principles calculations indicate that Cr addition increases formation energy of κ-carbides (e.g., Cr21(FeC3)2 formation energy -0.049 eV/atom vs. -0.160 eV/atom for ideal (Fe,Mn)3AlC), thereby suppressing nucleation and growth, while Ni addition reduces formation energy (AlNi3C formation energy -0.181 eV/atom), promoting κ-carbide formation.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: Comparing with other steels (Table 2, Fig. 4d), the present Ni&Cr-alloyed Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.5C steels achieve a superior combination of yield strength and total elongation, notably higher yield strength at similar GPa·% levels.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: The shear modulus difference between austenite matrix and κ-carbides is estimated as ΔG ≈ 23.3 GPa; the transition radius where dominant precipitation strengthening shifts from ordering to coherency+modulus mismatch is indicated in Fig. 10.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: Hall-Petch coefficient for these steels is taken as KHP = 446.8 MPa·μm^0.5; effective grain sizes (including twins) used for calculation are 3.51, 3.73, 3.72, and 2.2 μm respectively.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_8.value: Solute strengthening components calculated using empirical formula: 279[C] - 1.5[Mn] + 20.5[Al] + 2.9[Ni] + 3.9[Cr] (all in wt.%), yielding 515.4, 509.5, 557.2, 554.1 MPa for 3Cr, 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr, 2Ni5Cr respectively.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_9.value: Interrupted tensile tests performed at engineering strains of 3% (or 5%) and 10% to characterize microstructural evolution.

prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: The total elongation of 2Ni5Cr steel is ~16% higher than that of the no-Cr 2Ni steel, while its yield strength decreases from 1202 MPa (2Ni) to 1071 MPa.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: Compared to 3Cr steel, 2Ni3Cr steel shows a yield strength increase of ~141 MPa due to Ni addition.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Strain hardening curves (Fig. 4c) exhibit three stages: Stage I – sharp decrease in strain hardening rate; Stage II – increasing rate, prolonged with increasing Cr content among 2Ni/2Ni3Cr/2Ni5Cr; Stage III – decreasing rate, with 2Ni5Cr > 2Ni3Cr > 2Ni in work hardening rate.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Deformation substructure of 3Cr steel near final failure (54% strain) shows high-density dislocation arrays (HDDA), very fine slip bands, deformation twins, and stacking faults; twins appear only near fracture surfaces with limited contribution to overall ductility.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: 2Ni steel deformation is dominated by planar slip, slip band multiplication, and dynamic slip band refinement (DSBR); no deformation twins detected throughout plastic deformation; stacking faults appear only at very high strain near final failure.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: First-principles formation energies: (Fe,Mn)3AlC κ-carbide = -0.160 eV/atom; Cr21(FeC3)2 = -0.049 eV/atom (0.111 eV/atom higher than (Fe,Mn)3AlC); AlNi3C = -0.181 eV/atom; Fe2NiC = +0.082 eV/atom. Cr increases nucleation energy barrier, suppressing κ-carbide growth and favoring LRO domains; Ni reduces barrier, promoting κ-carbide formation via AlNi3C.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: Calculated strengthening contributions: σ0 = 42 MPa; σgb = 238.5 MPa (3Cr), 231.3 MPa (2Ni), 231.7 MPa (2Ni3Cr), 301.2 MPa (2Ni5Cr); σss = 515.4 MPa (3Cr), 509.5 MPa (2Ni), 557.2 MPa (2Ni3Cr), 554.1 MPa (2Ni5Cr); σprep varies with precipitate size distribution.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: TS × TEL values: 3Cr = 62.07 GPa-%, 2Ni = 43.75 GPa-%, 2Ni3Cr = 55.36 GPa-%, 2Ni5Cr = 59.94 GPa-%. The present steels distinguish themselves from Fe-Al, Fe-Mn, Fe-Mn-C, Fe-Mn-Al-C TRIPLEX, and stainless steels by significantly increased yield strength at equivalent TS×TEL (Fig. 4d and Table 2).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_8.value: Fracture surface SEM images (Fig. 5) show a large number of dimples in the fibre zone for all four steels, indicating ductile fracture mode.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_9.value: The interrupted tensile tests were performed to engineering strains of 3% (or 5%) and 10% to reveal microstructural evolution during straining; tensile specimens were cut along the rolling direction with gauge length 25 mm, thickness 3 mm, width 7 mm.
